title_en: Salah: The Complete Guide to Prayer
content_en:
Ibn Qudamah's treatment of salah in Al-Mughni is among the most comprehensive in classical Islamic literature. He addresses the conditions, pillars, obligations, Sunnahs, and nullifiers of prayer, as well as the detailed rules governing congregational prayer, the prayer of the traveler, the prayer during fear, the Friday prayer, the Eid prayers, and prayers for rain and solar eclipses.

The Hanbali school holds that the conditions for a valid prayer include: Islam, sanity, the age of discernment (a child of seven is to be instructed and one of ten disciplined), ritual purity, the entry of the prayer time, facing the qiblah, covering the awrah, and the prayer place and clothing being free of ritual impurity. The Hanbali school is strict on the question of ritual impurity on the prayer garment — if a Muslim prays in a garment bearing najasah that he was unaware of, he must repeat the prayer upon discovery, unlike the Maliki school which does not require this.

The Hanbali school identifies the pillars (arkan) of prayer as fourteen acts whose intentional or forgetful omission invalidates the prayer. These include: the standing position (qiyam) for those able, the opening takbir, recitation of al-Fatiha, bowing (ruku'), complete straightening after bowing (i'tidal), two prostrations (sujud) in each rakat, sitting between the two prostrations, the final tashahhud, sitting during the final tashahhud, the salawat on the Prophet in the tashahhud, the first taslim, and maintaining the sequence of all these acts.

Among the distinctive Hanbali positions: raising the hands (raf' al-yadayn) is Sunnah at the opening takbir, when bowing, when rising from bowing, and when rising from the first tashahhud — supporting the broader position held also by the Shafi'i school based on the authentic hadith of Ibn Umar (Bukhari). The Hanafi and Maliki schools restrict raising the hands to the opening takbir only.

The Hanbali school also holds that the opening supplication (du'a al-istiftah) is said quietly after the opening takbir before al-Fatiha, and that the Amin after al-Fatiha should be said aloud in prayers where the imam recites aloud — a point of agreement with the Shafi'i school.

Congregational prayer receives extensive treatment. Ibn Qudamah discusses the conditions for following an imam, the rulings on latecomer (masbuk) joining the prayer mid-rakat, the rules of the imam's prayer being invalid and its effect on followers, and the detailed positions on whether a follower must recite al-Fatiha or may rely on the imam's recitation. The Hanbali school holds that followers do not recite al-Fatiha in prayers where the imam recites aloud, relying on the hadith: "Whoever has an imam, the imam's recitation is his recitation" (Ibn Majah). This contrasts with the Shafi'i position requiring al-Fatiha from every worshipper in every rakat.

Ibn Qudamah also addresses the Friday prayer (Jumah) at length: its obligation falling on sane, adult, free male residents; the conditions for it to be valid (a minimum number of people — disputed among scholars); the two khutbahs that replace the two rakats of shortened Dhuhr; and the etiquette of the Friday. The comprehensiveness of this section reflects the importance of Jumah as the weekly communal act of worship for Muslim men.
title_id: Shalat: Panduan Lengkap tentang Ibadah Shalat
content_id:
Pembahasan shalat dalam Al-Mughni oleh Ibn Qudamah adalah salah satu yang paling komprehensif dalam literatur Islam klasik. Beliau membahas syarat-syarat, rukun-rukun, kewajiban-kewajiban, sunnah-sunnah, dan hal-hal yang membatalkan shalat secara sangat terperinci, disertai dalil-dalil dan perbandingan pendapat mazhab.

Topik-topik yang dibahas mencakup: syarat sahnya shalat (suci dari hadats dan najis, menutup aurat, menghadap kiblat, masuk waktu), adzan dan iqamat, imam dan makmum, shalat berjamaah dan hukumnya, shalat dalam berbagai kondisi (musafir, sakit, khauf/perang), shalat Jumat, shalat Id, shalat jenazah, shalat sunnah rawatib dan lainnya. Ibn Qudamah menghadirkan setiap masalah dengan kelengkapan dalil dan kejernihan analisis yang menjadikan Al-Mughni sebagai ensiklopedia shalat yang tidak tertandingi.
content_ar: Ibn Qudamah's treatment of الصلاة in Al-Mughni is among the most comprehensive in الكلاسيكي Islamic literature. He addresses the conditions, pillars, obligations, Sunnahs, and nullifiers of الصلاة, as well as the detailed rules governing congregational الصلاة, the الصلاة of the traveler, the الصلاة خلال الخوف, the Friday الصلاة, the Eid الصلوات, and الصلوات for rain and solar eclipses. The الحنبلي school holds that the conditions for a valid الصلاة include: الإسلام, sanity, the age of discernment (a الولد of seven is to be instructed and one of ten disciplined), ritual purity, the entry of the الصلاة time, facing the القبلة, covering the العورة, and the الصلاة place and clothing being free of ritual impurity. The الحنبلي school is strict on the question of ritual impurity on the الصلاة garment — إذا a مسلم prays in a garment bearing النجاسة that he was unaware of, he must repeat the الصلاة upon discovery, unlike the المالكي school which does not require this. The الحنبلي school identifies the pillars (arkan) of الصلاة as fourteen acts whose intentional or forgetful omission invalidates the الصلاة. These include: the standing position (qiyam) for those able, the opening التكبير, التلاوة of al-Fatiha, bowing (الركوع'), complete straightening بعد bowing (i'tidal), two prostrations (السجود) in each الركعات, sitting between the two prostrations, the final التشهد, sitting خلال the final التشهد, the salawat on النبي ﷺ in the التشهد, the أولاً التسليم, and maintaining the sequence of all these acts. Among the distinctive الحنبلي positions: raising the hands (raf' al-yadayn) is السنة at the opening التكبير, عندما bowing, عندما rising from bowing, and عندما rising from the أولاً التشهد — supporting the broader position held أيضاً by the الشافعي school based on the authentic الحديث of Ibn عمر (البخاري). The الحنفي and المالكي schools restrict raising the hands to the opening التكبير only. The الحنبلي school أيضاً holds that the opening الدعاء (الدعاء al-istiftah) is said quietly بعد the opening التكبير قبل al-Fatiha, and that the آمين بعد al-Fatiha should be said aloud in الصلوات where the الإمام recites aloud — a point of agreement with the الشافعي school. Congregational الصلاة receives extensive treatment. Ibn Qudamah discusses the conditions for following an الإمام, the rulings on latecomer (masbuk) joining the الصلاة mid-الركعات, the rules of the الإمام's الصلاة being invalid and its effect on followers, and the detailed positions on whether a follower must recite al-Fatiha or may rely on the الإمام's التلاوة. The الحنبلي school holds that followers do not recite al-Fatiha in الصلوات where the الإمام recites aloud, relying on the الحديث: "Whoever has an الإمام, the الإمام's التلاوة is his التلاوة" (ابن ماجه). This contrasts with the الشافعي position requiring al-Fatiha from every worshipper in every الركعات. Ibn Qudamah أيضاً addresses the Friday الصلاة (Jumah) at length: its الالتزام falling on san
